title: Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala
source_name: Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala
text:
Title: Kesavananda Bharati v State of Kerala
Citation: (1973) 4 SCC 225
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

The Supreme Court held that Parliament may amend the Constitution but cannot alter its basic structure. The basic structure doctrine protects constitutional identity, judicial review, rule of law, separation of powers, federalism, secularism, and fundamental rights from destructive amendments.

title: Maneka Gandhi v Union of India
source_name: Maneka Gandhi v Union of India
text:
Title: Maneka Gandhi v Union of India
Citation: (1978) 1 SCC 248
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

The Supreme Court expanded Article 21 by requiring procedure depriving life or personal liberty to be just, fair, and reasonable. The decision linked Articles 14, 19, and 21 and is foundational for substantive due process-like review in Indian constitutional law.

title: Minerva Mills v Union of India
source_name: Minerva Mills v Union of India
text:
Title: Minerva Mills v Union of India
Citation: (1980) 3 SCC 625
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

The Supreme Court reaffirmed the basic structure doctrine and held that limited amending power itself forms part of the basic structure. The case balances Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles as complementary constitutional commitments.

title: S.R. Bommai v Union of India
source_name: S.R. Bommai v Union of India
text:
Title: S.R. Bommai v Union of India
Citation: (1994) 3 SCC 1
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

The Court made presidential proclamations under Article 356 subject to judicial review and reinforced federalism and secularism as constitutional principles. The case is central to emergency powers, state government dismissal, and constitutional federalism.

title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v Union of India
source_name: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v Union of India
text:
Title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v Union of India
Citation: (2017) 10 SCC 1
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

A nine-judge bench recognized privacy as a fundamental right under the Constitution. Puttaswamy is central to dignity, autonomy, informational privacy, proportionality, data protection, surveillance, and constitutional limits on state action.

title: Navtej Singh Johar v Union of India
source_name: Navtej Singh Johar v Union of India
text:
Title: Navtej Singh Johar v Union of India
Citation: (2018) 10 SCC 1
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

The Supreme Court decriminalized consensual same-sex relations by reading down Section 377 of the Penal Code. The case is important for equality, dignity, privacy, constitutional morality, transformative constitutionalism, and anti-discrimination principles.

title: D.K. Basu v State of West Bengal
source_name: D.K. Basu v State of West Bengal
text:
Title: D.K. Basu v State of West Bengal
Citation: (1997) 1 SCC 416
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

The Supreme Court laid down safeguards for arrest and detention, including transparency about arrest, notification, medical examination, records, and protection against custodial abuse. It remains one of the leading Indian authorities on arrest procedure, police accountability, and detainee rights.

title: Joginder Kumar v State of U.P.
source_name: Joginder Kumar v State of U.P.
text:
Title: Joginder Kumar v State of U.P.
Citation: (1994) 4 SCC 260
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

The Supreme Court stressed that arrest power does not justify routine arrest and that liberty requires a rational justification for taking a person into custody. The case is often cited on the limits of arrest discretion, the need for grounds, and the obligation to inform relatives or friends of the arrest.

title: Arnesh Kumar v State of Bihar
source_name: Arnesh Kumar v State of Bihar
text:
Title: Arnesh Kumar v State of Bihar
Citation: (2014) 8 SCC 273
Jurisdiction: india
Source URL: https://main.sci.gov.in/

The Supreme Court reinforced limits on unnecessary arrest and directed careful compliance with statutory safeguards before remand. It is a practical leading case on arrest discipline, liberty-protective procedure, and judicial scrutiny of routine custody requests.
